final_answer: Q2: N5 first activates at T=1.
Q3: If the link N1->N3 is removed, N3, N4, and N5 would never activate.
Q4: If N2's delay becomes 0, N4 activates at T=1.
working: At T=0, N1 sends simultaneously to N2 and N3.
Assume receipt by N2 and N3 occurs at T=0, and forwarding delays are measured from that receipt time.
N3 has a 1-step delay, so it sends to N4 and N5 at T=1.
Therefore N5 receives from N3 at T=1 and first activates at T=1.
N2 has a 2-step delay, so it sends to N4 at T=2.
N4 needs messages from both N2 and N3, and activates when the second one arrives.
N4 receives from N3 at T=1 and from N2 at T=2, so the second arrival is at T=2; therefore N4 activates at T=2.
When N4 activates, it sends to N5 with no delay, so N5 also receives from N4 at T=2, but its first activation already occurred at T=1.
Counterfactual removing N1->N3: N3 never receives any message, so N3 never activates/forwards.
Without N3's message, N4 never gets both required inputs, so N4 never activates.
Without N3 and without N4 activating, N5 receives nothing and never activates.
Counterfactual with N2 delay = 0: N2 forwards to N4 at T=0, while N3 still forwards to N4 at T=1.
Then N4 has inputs from N2 at T=0 and N3 at T=1, so it activates when the second arrives, at T=1.

To achieve 9, both machines would need to be packed very tightly with little idle time, but T5 cannot start until both T3 and T4 finish. Since T3 depends on T1, the earliest T3 can finish is 7 if T1 starts at 0. Thus T5 earliest finish is 9.

Now also T6 (3 hours) must occur after T4, and T4 depends on T2. If T2 runs 0-2 and T4 runs 2-3, then T6 earliest is 3-6. This fits, but total machine loading matters: besides T1+T3+T5 = 9 hours, the remaining T2+T4+T6 = 6 hours. Since T5 must start at 7 earliest, one machine is effectively occupied through 9 by T1,T3,T5 if arranged tightly.

But there is still 15 total hours of work. To finish by 9, the two machines would need exactly 18 capacity-hours over [0,9], leaving only 3 idle hours total. Because T5 cannot start before 7 and only lasts 2 hours, one machine must carry T1 and T3 before it, using 7 hours exactly. The other machine must fit T2,T4,T6 and also any remaining tasks, but there are none. This seems feasible in principle, so test explicitly.

Attempt 9-hour schedule: put T1 0-3, T3 3-7, T5 7-9 on one machine; put T2 0-2, T4 2-3, T6 3-6 on the other. Then all listed tasks finish by 9, but note total work is 15, and this schedule indeed uses 9 hours on M1 and 6 hours on M2, so 9 is feasible.
However, the task list also includes six tasks only, and this schedule completes all six by 9. Rechecking overlap and dependencies confirms validity.
Therefore the optimal makespan is 9 hours, with the given schedule.
